sections.paragraph:
πάλιν [etym. complex] adv. The basic function is one of explicit or implicit connection with a datum in a narrative structure, with focus on repetitive or alternate aspect relative either to the present or the past.

—a. marker of an additional occurrence or datum

—α. again, once more Mt 13:45; 22:4; 26:42; Mk 2:13; Lk 13:20; 23:20 (s. b); J 1:35; 4:13, 54; 9:15; 11:38; 12:28; 18:27, 38; Ac 10:15; 17:32 (another time); 2 Cor 2:1; 3:1; Gal 1:9; 2:18; Phil 2:28; Hb 5:12; Js 5:18; 2 Pt 2:20; 1 J 2:8; Rv 10:8, 11. εἰς τὸ πάλιν = π. 2 Cor 13:2. In scriptural citation Mt 4:7; J 12:39; 19:37; Ro 15:10-12; 1 Cor 3:20; Hb 1:5; 2:13; 4:5; 10:30.

—β. on the other hand, in turn Mt 4:7; Lk 6:43; 1 Cor 12:21; 2 Cor 10:7.

—γ. what’s more, moreover Mt 5:33; 19:24. In close linguistic association is the use in

—b. marker of reversion back Mk 5:21; J 10:18; 11:7; Ro 11:23; 1 Cor 7:5; 2 Cor 1:16; Gal 1:17; 2:1; 4:9; Phil 1:26. πάλιν εἰς φόβον = to go back to fear Ro 8:15. Since a verbal encounter constitutes a back-and-forth pattern, π. w. verbs expressing outcry may be rendered they shouted back (the idea remains the same if it is assumed that the writer had more than one volley of words in mind) Mk 15:13; J 18:40; cp. Lk 23:20 (s. aα).
